Option B is incorrect because one delta is characteristic of a Loop. Option C is incorrect because two deltas are characteristic of a Whorl. Option D and E are incorrect because deltas are limited by the pattern geometry, and arches specifically lack them.

questions.question: A medical examiner needs to record the fingerprints of a deceased individual whose hands are tightly clenched due to advanced rigor mortis. What is the most appropriate technique to use?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because a "fingerprint spoon" or a curved strip of paper is specifically designed to be inserted into the clenched hand of a cadaver in rigor mortis to obtain a legible print without damaging the body. Option A is incorrect because it is unethical and unnecessary to mutilate the body. Option C is incorrect because this is a last-resort technique for mummified or macerated bodies, not simple rigor. Option D is incorrect because waiting may allow putrefaction to set in, destroying the ridges. Option E is incorrect because boiling water is used to "plump up" shriveled or mummified skin, not to resolve rigor.

options.A: Forcefully breaking the rigor by snapping the tendons
options.B: Using a "fingerprint spoon" or spatula to slide the paper into the palm
options.C: Cutting the skin of the fingertips and flattening it under glass
options.D: Waiting 48 hours for the rigor to pass naturally
options.E: Injecting the fingertips with boiling water to soften the tissue

questions.question: A suspect is accused of forging a thumb impression on a property document. On microscopic examination of the print, the forensic expert notes that the sweat pores are perfectly circular and spaced with mathematical regularity. What does this finding suggest?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because in a genuine fingerprint, sweat pores are irregularly spaced and vary slightly in shape; mathematical regularity and perfect circularity are hallmarks of a mechanical forgery, such as a rubber stamp or a printed reproduction. Option A is incorrect because adermatoglyphia is the total absence of ridges. Option B is incorrect because genuine prints are never mathematically perfect. Option C is incorrect because gloves would either leave no print or a fabric pattern, not a perfect ridge-pore pattern. Option E is incorrect because dermatitis would distort or obliterate pores, not make them more regular.

options.A: The suspect has a rare genetic condition called adermatoglyphia
options.B: The print is a genuine "chance impression"
options.C: The suspect was wearing thin latex gloves during the act
options.D: The print is a forgery made by a rubber stamp
options.E: The suspect has chronic dermatitis affecting the pore distribution

questions.question: A forensic anthropologist is examining a sternum recovered from a site. They observe that the junctions between the second, third, and fourth segments of the sternal body are completely obliterated by bone, but the junction between the first and second segments is still clearly visible and unfused. What is the most likely age of this individual?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the segments of the sternal body (mesosternum) fuse from below upwards; the union between the third and fourth segments occurs at puberty (~15 years), the union between the second and third segments occurs by age 20, and the final union between the first and second segments occurs between ages 21 and 25. Option A is incorrect because at age 10–12, the segments of the sternal body are still separated by primary cartilaginous joints. Option B is incorrect because at age 14–16, typically only the lowest junction (between the third and fourth segments) has completed fusion. Option D is incorrect because by age 28–32, all segments of the sternal body are fused into a single unit, and the xiphoid process may have already begun its fusion to the body. Option E is incorrect because this age range is associated with the fusion of the xiphoid process to the body (around age 40), whereas the internal segments of the body fuse much earlier in young adulthood.

questions.question: Which carpal bone is the last to develop an ossification center in the human wrist?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the pisiform is the last of the carpal bones to ossify, with its ossification center typically appearing between the ages of 9 and 12 years. Option A is incorrect because the capitate is the very first carpal bone to ossify, usually appearing within the first few months of infancy. Option B is incorrect because the hamate ossifies shortly after the capitate, typically appearing by the end of the first year of life. Option C is incorrect because the lunate ossification center generally appears between the ages of 3 and 4 years. Option D is incorrect because the trapezoid typically begins to ossify between the ages of 4 and 6 years, significantly earlier than the pisiform.
